---
title: "Clustering Effects on Epidemic Dynamics"
author: "Simon Frost"
date: today
subtitle: "NodeBasedModels.jl Vignette 08 (clustering effects)"
---

## Introduction

Triangles in a contact network — three mutual contacts $A$–$B$–$C$ all linked — slow down epidemics because once two of the three are infected, the third has fewer naive susceptible neighbours to spread to. The pair-approximation framework captures this through the **clustering coefficient** $\phi$ and a triangle-aware closure (Keeling, Barnard, or Eames).

This vignette quantifies the effect by sweeping $\phi$ from $0$ to $0.6$.

## Setup

```{julia}
using NodeBasedModels
using Plots
```

## Sweep

```{julia}
# Universal anchors: γ=0.25, R₀=2, β derived per scenario (see plan.md)
γ_anchor = 0.25
R0_target = 2.0
n_degree = 6
T_anchor = R0_target / (n_degree - 1)   # T = R₀/(k-1) = 0.4
τ_anchor = T_anchor * γ_anchor / (1 - T_anchor)  # 1/6

function final_size(ϕ; closure = KeelingClosure(), τ = τ_anchor, γ = γ_anchor)
    net = regular_network(n_degree; ϕ = ϕ)
    psys = generate_pairwise(sir_model(), net, closure;
                             tspan = (0.0, 40.0), N = 1.0,
                             seed_fraction = 0.01)
    p = copy(psys.params); p[:τ] = τ; p[:γ] = γ
    sol  = solve_pairwise(psys, p; reltol = 1e-8, abstol = 1e-10)
    return sol[psys.singles[:R]][end]
end

ϕgrid = 0.0:0.05:0.6
R∞_keeling = [final_size(ϕ; closure = KeelingClosure()) for ϕ in ϕgrid]
R∞_barnard = [final_size(ϕ; closure = BarnardClosure()) for ϕ in ϕgrid]
nothing
```

```{julia}
plot(ϕgrid, R∞_keeling, label = "Keeling", lw = 2, marker = :circle)
plot!(ϕgrid, R∞_barnard, label = "Barnard", lw = 2, marker = :diamond)
xlabel!("Clustering coefficient ϕ")
ylabel!("Final attack size R(∞)")
title!("Triangle density reduces final size, 6-regular SIR (R₀ = 2 at ϕ=0)")
```

As clustering grows, both Keeling and Barnard predict a smaller epidemic. The Bernoulli closure (no clustering correction) would predict the same final size at every $\phi$ — see vignette 02 for the comparison at a single $\phi$.

## Closures diverge as $\phi$ grows

```{julia}
R∞_bernoulli = [final_size(ϕ; closure = BernoulliClosure()) for ϕ in ϕgrid]
plot(ϕgrid, R∞_bernoulli, label = "Bernoulli (ignores clustering)",
     lw = 2, ls = :dash)
plot!(ϕgrid, R∞_keeling, label = "Keeling", lw = 2)
plot!(ϕgrid, R∞_barnard, label = "Barnard", lw = 2)
xlabel!("ϕ"); ylabel!("R(∞)")
title!("Closure choice matters when ϕ > 0")
```

## Summary

Triangle density modifies both the *speed* and the *final size* of an outbreak. Use Keeling, Barnard, or Eames closures whenever the network has $\phi > 0.1$ or so; reserve Bernoulli for the unclustered limit.

## Simulation validation

We validate the pair-approximation against Gillespie SSA at two
clustering levels: $\phi = 0$ (unclustered, Bernoulli closure) and
$\phi = 0.3$ (moderate clustering, Keeling closure). The SSA uses
`clustered_regular_graph_builder` which applies triangle-forming
degree-preserving edge swaps on a $k$-regular graph to achieve
the target $\phi$.

```{julia}
include("../_validation.jl")

N_v = 1000
prog_no = sir_model(τ = :β)
params_no = Dict(:β => τ_anchor, :γ => γ_anchor)

# --- ϕ = 0 (unclustered) ---
t_uc, μ_uc, σ_uc = gillespie_ribbon(prog_no, params_no,
    regular_graph_builder(N_v, n_degree);
    N = N_v, n_graphs = 5, nsims_per_graph = 40,
    tspan = (0.0, 40.0), seed_fraction = 0.01,
    tgrid = collect(0.0:0.5:40.0))

psys_uc = generate_pairwise(sir_model(), regular_network(n_degree, ϕ = 0.0),
                             BernoulliClosure();
                             tspan = (0.0, 40.0), N = 1.0, seed_fraction = 0.01)
p_uc = copy(psys_uc.params); p_uc[:τ] = τ_anchor; p_uc[:γ] = γ_anchor
sol_uc = solve_pairwise(psys_uc, p_uc; reltol = 1e-8, abstol = 1e-10)

# --- ϕ ≈ 0.2 (clustered) ---
# Build one clustered graph, measure its actual ϕ, then use that in the PA.
ϕ_target = 0.2
cl_builder = clustered_regular_graph_builder(N_v, n_degree, ϕ_target; max_iters = 500_000)
g_sample = cl_builder(StableRNG(20240501))
ϕ_actual = Graphs.global_clustering_coefficient(g_sample)
println("Clustered graph: target ϕ=$ϕ_target, achieved ϕ=$(round(ϕ_actual; digits=3))")

t_cl, μ_cl, σ_cl = gillespie_ribbon(prog_no, params_no,
    cl_builder;
    N = N_v, n_graphs = 3, nsims_per_graph = 60,
    tspan = (0.0, 40.0), seed_fraction = 0.01,
    tgrid = collect(0.0:0.5:40.0))

psys_cl = generate_pairwise(sir_model(), regular_network(n_degree, ϕ = ϕ_actual),
                             KeelingClosure();
                             tspan = (0.0, 40.0), N = 1.0, seed_fraction = 0.01)
p_cl = copy(psys_cl.params); p_cl[:τ] = τ_anchor; p_cl[:γ] = γ_anchor
sol_cl = solve_pairwise(psys_cl, p_cl; reltol = 1e-8, abstol = 1e-10)
```

```{julia}
#| label: fig-nbm-08-validation
#| fig-cap: "Pair-approximation I(t) vs SSA at two clustering levels. Left: ϕ=0 (Bernoulli closure); right: ϕ≈0.3 (Keeling closure at the graph's measured ϕ). N=1000, k=6, R₀=2 at ϕ=0."
p1 = plot(sol_uc.t, sol_uc[psys_uc.singles[:I]],
          label = "PA (Bernoulli)", lw = 2, color = :blue,
          ylabel = "Fraction infected", title = "ϕ = 0 (unclustered)",
          ylims = (0, 0.35))
plot!(p1, t_uc, μ_uc[:I], ribbon = σ_uc[:I],
      label = "SSA", color = :blue, fillalpha = 0.2, linealpha = 0.4, lw = 1)

p2 = plot(sol_cl.t, sol_cl[psys_cl.singles[:I]],
          label = "PA (Keeling)", lw = 2, color = :red,
          title = "ϕ ≈ $(round(ϕ_actual; digits=2)) (clustered)",
          ylims = (0, 0.35))
plot!(p2, t_cl, μ_cl[:I], ribbon = σ_cl[:I],
      label = "SSA", color = :red, fillalpha = 0.2, linealpha = 0.4, lw = 1)

plot(p1, p2, layout = (1, 2), size = (800, 350),
     xlabel = "Time")
```

The Bernoulli closure matches the unclustered SSA at $\phi = 0$. At
$\phi = 0.3$, clustering reduces the epidemic; the Keeling closure
(which accounts for the triangle density) tracks the clustered SSA
mean, confirming that the closure correction captures the dominant
effect of short cycles on SIR dynamics.
